key_departures.impact: Williams announced his retirement, placing him on the Reserve/Retired list. A 6-year veteran who spent 6 of 8 NFL seasons with the Rams and was part of the Super Bowl LVI roster, he was declining and his departure was expected. Saves approximately $7.5M in cap space.

Durant, a fan favorite known for his ball-hawking ability, signed a 1-year prove-it deal with the Cowboys worth up to $5.5M. Was arguably the most dependable DB on the Rams in 2025 before the McDuffie trade displaced him. His departure further thins CB depth behind McDuffie and Watson.

McCreary, acquired mid-season via trade, signed a 1-year deal with Detroit. Slot-versatile corner who played well in limited reps during the playoffs. Departure strips CB depth but is mitigated by McDuffie and Watson additions.

Witherspoon missed most of the 2025 season with multiple clavicle injuries. Became a UFA and has not been re-signed by the Rams. The team appears to have moved on given his injury history and the CB overhaul with McDuffie and Watson.

Longtime starting right tackle Havenstein retired this offseason. His departure creates a genuine need at right tackle. The Rams only re-signed depth player David Quessenberry (1yr deal) to bridge the gap, leaving RT as an unresolved starter-level hole heading into the draft.
Humphries, a veteran swing tackle, did not return. His departure is low-impact given Quessenberry's re-signing fills the depth role, but further underscores the thin OT depth behind Alaric Jackson.

Atwell, a former second-round pick who never fully lived up to his draft status despite a 1-year, $10M deal in 2025, has not been re-signed. His departure is a moderate loss at WR depth/speed element but leaves WR as a positional concern behind Puka Nacua and Davante Adams.

Garoppolo, the backup QB who threw only a handful of passes over two seasons in LA, is unsigned. The Cardinals have been reported as interested. His potential departure creates a need for a QB2 but is a low-stakes positional need.
The veteran long snapper who bridged the special teams chaos mid-season was not re-signed. The Rams replaced him with Joe Cardona. No need created.

contract.notes: 4-year extension signed upon trade completion. Trade cost: 2026 1st (No. 29), 2026 5th, 2026 6th, 2027 3rd. Extension makes McDuffie the highest-paid CB in NFL history at $31M AAV.

signings.analysis: All-Pro CB acquired via blockbuster trade and immediately locked up on a record-setting $124M/4yr extension with $100M guaranteed — the largest guarantee ever for a cornerback. McDuffie, 25, is a 2x Super Bowl champion, 2x All-Pro (1st team 2023, 2nd team 2024) and was the centerpiece signing the Rams needed. The $100M fully guaranteed at signing is the clearest possible signal: this is their CB1 for the long term. CB need fully addressed at the starter level with massive commitment. Rams traded away picks 29, 5th, 6th (2026) and a 2027 3rd to get him.

contract.notes: Signed as UFA. Watson is McDuffie's former Chiefs teammate, reuniting them in LA.

signings.analysis: Watson follows his ex-teammate McDuffie to LA on a 3yr/$51M deal with $34M guaranteed — a legitimate starter-tier contract. Coming off a career year in 2025 (career highs in snaps, interceptions, tackles), Watson is expected to start opposite McDuffie. Three years of guaranteed money signals a real commitment as CB2. Combined with McDuffie, the Rams have overhauled their entire CB1/CB2 situation. CB is no longer a top-tier draft need.

contract.notes: Max value $39M with incentives. $18.75M fully guaranteed at signing per OverTheCap. $7M of 2027 salary guaranteed.

signings.analysis: Curl re-signed before hitting free agency on a 3yr/$36M deal with $24M guaranteed. Coming off his best season (122 tackles, 2 INTs regular season, 1 playoff INT that sent the Rams to the NFC Championship), he is the starting strong safety alongside Quentin Lake (already extended in January). Safety was a potential need if Curl walked; re-signing him at starter money eliminates it entirely. The S pairing of Lake and Curl is locked up with real contractual commitment from both.

contract.notes: Contract extension signed in January 2026 before the official FA period. Total value and guaranteed figures not publicly disclosed. Lake re-signed before free agency opened, locking up the safety position alongside Curl.

signings.analysis: Lake's extension in January 2026 secured the starting safety duo before free agency began. Combined with Curl's re-signing, the Rams have both starting safeties under multi-year deals. S is not a draft need.

contract.notes: $8M represents max value per agent; guarantees not publicly disclosed but expected to be minimal given age and injury history.

signings.analysis: Higbee's re-signing at $4M AAV (max $8M/2yr) keeps the 13-personnel-heavy offense intact. McVay's 2025 offense leaned heavily on three-TE sets (13 personnel) with Higbee, Colby Parkinson, Davis Allen, and Terrance Ferguson all returning. Higbee is a low-cost depth/rotational contributor who missed 7 games in 2025 and 14 in 2024 due to injuries. At 33, this is bridge money — partially addresses TE depth but is not a significant financial commitment. TE is not a pressing need given the deep group.

contract.notes: One-year deal; financial terms not disclosed. Thomas spent 2025 on injured reserve.

signings.analysis: Thomas is returning for a 5th season on a 1-year deal after spending all of 2025 on IR. Depth/rotational EDGE signing with minimal cap impact. Does not address any starter-level EDGE need and is essentially a camp body with some familiarity. Jared Verse is the team's primary young EDGE rusher under contract.

contract.notes: 2-year deal reported at approximately $3.45M max value. Replaces Jake McQuaide.

signings.analysis: Cardona is an 11-year veteran who overlapped with new special teams coordinator Bubba Ventrone in New England (2015-2017). The Rams' special teams were an Achilles heel in 2025 — they changed kickers, long snappers, and fired the ST coordinator mid-season. Cardona stabilizes the long-snapper position at fair market value. Special teams need addressed.

contract.notes: 2-year deal worth up to $5.05M per Aaron Wilson/KPRC2. Former Lions special teams captain.

signings.analysis: Stuard is primarily a special teams specialist — 18th overall special teamer by PFF in 2025, team-high 375 special teams snaps with Detroit. The Rams' special teams were among the worst in the NFL in 2025. Stuard provides elite coverage-unit play and depth LB value but does not address any off-ball linebacker starter need. Nate Landman and Omar Speights remain the projected starters. LB depth concern minimally addressed; starting LB need unchanged.

contract.notes: Re-signed February 2026 before official FA period on a 1-year deal. Estimated at approximately $1.33M based on his 2025 contract; 2026 terms not officially disclosed.

signings.analysis: Quessenberry's re-signing is a 1-year veteran minimum-tier deal for a swing tackle who primarily played special teams in 2025. He does NOT address the starter-level void left by Rob Havenstein's retirement. His return allows the Rams to avoid a desperate OT pick early in the draft, but a starting-quality RT is still needed. OT remains a significant draft need.

contract.notes: Tendered as exclusive rights free agent at league minimum. Cannot negotiate with other teams.

signings.analysis: Mevis was one of the surprises of the Rams' 2025 season — 12/13 FGs and all 39 PATs in 9 regular-season games, plus 6/6 FGs and 9/9 PATs in three playoff games including a game-winning OT FG vs. the Bears. The ERFA tender locks him up at the minimum for another year. Kicker is resolved.

contract.notes: Tendered as exclusive rights free agent at league minimum.

signings.analysis: Dedich appeared in 32 games over 2 seasons with 10 starts. Cheap ERFA retention keeps interior depth intact. Does not address any starting IOL need — Steve Avila and Kevin Dotson are the starters, both entering the final year of their contracts.

contract.notes: Tendered as exclusive rights free agent at league minimum. Primary punt returner in 2025.

signings.analysis: Smith posted career highs with 18 catches for 303 yards in 2025. The ERFA tender retains him cheaply as WR depth and a special teams contributor. He had a critical muffed punt in the NFC Championship game. His retention does not address the WR depth/starter need — Puka Nacua and Davante Adams (age 33) remain WR1/2, but the position behind them is thin with Atwell departed.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: The Rams completely overhauled their CB room with two blockbuster moves: Trent McDuffie (4yr/$124M, $100M gtd — record-setting, clear CB1) and Jaylen Watson (3yr/$51M, $34M gtd — legitimate CB2 commitment). Both are 25 years old. Three CBs departed (Williams retired, Durant to Dallas, McCreary to Detroit, Witherspoon unsigned) but the incoming pair is categorically superior. CB drops from a critical tier-1 need to a tier-4 luxury — depth corner in Day 3 at most.

Quentin Lake extended in January and Kamren Curl re-signed on a 3yr/$36M deal with $24M guaranteed. Both starting safeties are locked up under multi-year contracts. S is no longer a draft need whatsoever.

Rob Havenstein's retirement CREATED a genuine starting RT need that did not exist before. The only OT move was re-signing 35-year-old David Quessenberry on a ~$1.33M depth deal — he is not a starter solution. DJ Humphries also departed unsigned. The Rams need a legitimate starting RT, making this a top-2 remaining draft need heading into April. Pick No. 13 is in play for an OT prospect.

Tutu Atwell departed without being replaced by a meaningful signing. Behind Puka Nacua (locked in) and Davante Adams (33 years old, $28M cap hit), the room is thin with only Jordan Whittington, Konata Mumpfield, and Xavier Smith (ERFA). WR remains a tier-2 need. Most mock drafts project the Rams taking a WR at pick 13 in April.

Jimmy Garoppolo is unsigned. If he does not return, the Rams would head into the draft with Matthew Stafford (38) as the only quarterback under contract. QB2 is now a mild need that could be addressed in the mid-rounds of the draft or via a late free agency signing. Escalated from a non-issue to a tier-3 concern.

Grant Stuard signed (2yr/$5.05M) as a special teams specialist with LB depth upside. Nate Landman and Omar Speights return as starters. Troy Reeder departed unsigned. Stuard's signing is primarily a special teams upgrade — LB starting depth remains a concern but is not yet at crisis level. Need unchanged at tier 3.

No IOL moves were made except ERFA tenders for Dedich (depth). Steve Avila and Kevin Dotson are starters on expiring contracts entering 2026. The Rams are approaching a decision point — both guards need extensions or replacements. IOL need unchanged as a mid-draft priority.

Higbee re-signed on a 2yr/$8M deal (max value) and all four primary TEs from 2025 (Higbee, Parkinson, Davis Allen, Ferguson) return. The 13-personnel heavy offense continues intact. TE drops from a tier-3 need to tier-4 luxury — drafting a developmental TE is possible but not pressing.

key_additions.headline: NFL's new highest-paid CB ($31M AAV, 2x All-Pro) gives Rams an elite CB1 to anchor a rebuilt secondary
McDuffie's former Chiefs running mate reunites with him in LA as a legitimate CB2 on a starter-money deal
Career-best season (122 tackles, 3 INTs including a playoff OT winner) earns Curl a long-term commitment as starting safety
Elite special teams ace (18th-ranked ST player per PFF, 375 ST snaps in 2025) addresses a major Rams weakness
11-year veteran long snapper stabilizes a special teams unit that was a liability throughout 2025

key_losses.headline: Starting RT of 10+ years retires, leaving an immediate hole at right tackle that depth signing Quessenberry cannot fill
6-year Ram retires, but his departure is softened by the arrivals of McDuffie and Watson
Fan-favorite and former starter reads the writing on the wall after McDuffie trade and signs prove-it deal in Dallas
Trade acquisition who shined in the playoffs departs on a 1-year deal as CB room overhaul renders him expendable
Former 2nd-round pick exits after underwhelming tenure, leaving WR depth behind Nacua and Adams thin
